coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of corn straw-based graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and their application in PO4 3- detection
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yin
He, Qinqin
Zhao, Xin
Yuan, Junjun
Zhao, Hongjun
Wang, Gongting
Li, Min
coredata.dc:description: Phosphate (PO4 3-) can cause eutrophication of water bodies due to soil erosion and agricultural fertilization, increasing toxic substances in water, therefore detecting PO4 3- is vital in water eutrophication. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), zero-dimensional nanometer-sized graphene pieces, are made of a few graphene lattices and exhibit outstanding optical properties. The traditional synthesis precursors are limited by large carbon domains or toxic polycyclic aromatic molecules. Consequently, synthesis of GQDs from renewable biomass has attracted broad interests. Herein corn straw was used to directly prepare GQDs via a one-pot hydrothermal strategy. The as-prepared GQDs, with an average size of 2.67 nm, presented high quantum yield (15.67%), high crystallinity and dispersibility. The as-prepared non-doped GQDs had a special selectivity to Ce4+/Fe3+, causing the fluorescence (FL) quenching of GQDs and the formation of GQDs-Ce4+/GQDs-Fe3+ aggregation. In the presence of PO4 3-, a strong affinity between PO4 3- and Ce4+/Fe3+ can destroy GQDs-Ce4+/GQDs-Fe3+ aggregation, resulting in the FL recovery of GQDs. The GQDs-Ce4+ probe system can selectively detect PO4 3- with a detection limit of 0.06 μM in a linear range from 0.1 to 20.0 μM. Based on this, GQDs-Ce4+ paper-based sensors were also prepared for the visual detection of PO4 3-, which showed good responses to PO4 3-. This development is expected to be a promising strategy for convenient and rapid phosphate detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass derived GQDs
Hydrothermal treatment
Phosphate detection
Fluorescent sensing
Biomass conversion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of PO4 3- in the real water sample
Morphological and structural properties of GQDs
Determination of PO4 3- in the real water sample
Detection of PO4 3- by GQDs-Ce4+ paper-based sensors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology of GQDs. (a) TEM image of the synthesized GQDs, the inset figure is the size distribution histogram of GQDs. (b, d) HRTEM image of GQDs, and a white lattice line profile on the GQDs. (c) FFT pattern of GQDs. (e) AFM image of GQDs. (f) The height profile image of GQDs. (g) AFM image of aggregated GQDs-Ce4+. (h) The height profile image of GQDs-Ce4+ (the black line and the red line respectively represent the height profile of the left and right line in the AFM image of GQDs-Ce4+).(For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) XRD spectra of the synthesized GQDs and corn straw powders. (b) FTIR spectra of GQDs and corn straw powders. (c) XPS survey spectra of GQDs. (d) C1s high-resolution spectra of GQDs. (d) O1s high-resolution spectra of GQDs.

(a) GQDs aqueous solution under daylight. (b) GQDs aqueous solution under the UV light of 365 nm. (c) GQDs powders. (d) UV–vis, FL excitation and emission spectra of GQDs aqueous solution. (e) FL emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths from 330 to 440 nm with a 10 nm increment. (f) Normalized FL spectra at different pH values, inset: the plot of fluorescence intensities at 450 nm at different pH values.

(a) The relative fluorescent intensities (F/F0) of GQDs in the presence of 1 mM different metal ions. F and F0 were the fluorescence intensity of GQDs in the presence and absence of metal ions. (b) Normalized fluorescent spectra of the GQDs with adding different concentrations of Ce4+ (0–2 mM), the inset figure is the relative fluorescence intensities (F/F0) of GQDs added with Ce4+ ions at excitation wavelength 370 nm and emission wavelength 450 nm. (c) Normalized fluorescent spectra of the GQDs with adding different concentrations of Fe3+ (0–2 mM), the inset figure is the relative fluorescence intensities (F/F0) of GQDs added with Fe3+ ions at excitation wavelength 370 nm and emission wavelength 450 nm.
CV of GQDs, GQDs-Ce4+, GQDs-Fe3+, Ce4+-PO4 3-and Fe3+-PO4 3- for determination of LUMO and HOMO energy levels.

(a) Normalized FL recovery spectra collected from adding 0–100 μM PO4 3- to the GQDs-Ce4+ solution. (b) F/F0 values of GQDs-Ce4+ added with different concentrations of PO4 3- at excitation wavelength 450 nm, F represented the fluorescence intensity of GQDs-Ce4+ in the presence of PO4 3-, F0 is the initial fluorescence intensity of GQDs, the inset figure displayed the GQDs-Ce4+ responses to PO4 3- at low concentrations of 0.1–20 μM. (c) Normalized FL recovery spectra collected from adding 0–100 μM PO4 3- to the GQDs-Fe3+ solution. (d) F/F0 values of GQDs-Fe3+ added with different concentrations of PO4 3- at excitation wavelength 450 nm, F represented the fluorescence intensity of GQDs-Fe3+ in the presence of PO4 3-, F0 is the fluorescence intensity of GQDs, the inset figure displayed the GQDs-Fe3+ responses to PO4 3- at low concentrations of 0.1–20 μM.
The fluorescence recovering pattern of different concentrations of PO4 3- on the GQDs-Ce4+ paper-based sensor (under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp irradiating).
(a) the synthesis of GQDs from corn straw powders, (b) the selective detection of PO4 3- using GQDs-Ce4+ or GQDs-Fe3+ strategy.
Comparison of the quantum yield with other reported GQDs.
LUMO/HOMO and Eg of GQDs, GQDs-Ce4+, GQDs-Fe3+, Ce4+-PO4 3- and Fe3+-PO4 3-.
The comparison of the determination of PO4 3- by GQDs.
Comparison of three methods for detecting PO4 3- in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of corn straw-based graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and their application in PO4 3- detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Beijing Forestry University, Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Beijing Forestry University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Environmental Science and Engineering, Beijing Forestry University, Beijing 100083, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Lab for Source Control Technology of Water Pollution. Beijing Forestry University, Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Lab for Source Control Technology of Water Pollution. Beijing Forestry University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Key Lab for Source Control Technology of Water Pollution. Beijing Forestry University, Beijing 100083, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: College of Environmental Science and Engineering Beijing Forestry University, 60# 35# Qinghua, East Rd., Haidian District, Beijing 100083, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering Beijing Forestry University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Phosphate (PO4 3-) can cause eutrophication of water bodies due to soil erosion and agricultural fertilization, increasing toxic substances in water, therefore detecting PO4 3- is vital in water eutrophication. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), zero-dimensional nanometer-sized graphene pieces, are made of a few graphene lattices and exhibit outstanding optical properties. The traditional synthesis precursors are limited by large carbon domains or toxic polycyclic aromatic molecules. Consequently, synthesis of GQDs from renewable biomass has attracted broad interests. Herein corn straw was used to directly prepare GQDs via a one-pot hydrothermal strategy. The as-prepared GQDs, with an average size of 2.67 nm, presented high quantum yield (15.67%), high crystallinity and dispersibility. The as-prepared non-doped GQDs had a special selectivity to Ce4+/Fe3+, causing the fluorescence (FL) quenching of GQDs and the formation of GQDs-Ce4+/GQDs-Fe3+ aggregation. In the presence of PO4 3-, a strong affinity between PO4 3- and Ce4+/Fe3+ can destroy GQDs-Ce4+/GQDs-Fe3+ aggregation, resulting in the FL recovery of GQDs. The GQDs-Ce4+ probe system can selectively detect PO4 3- with a detection limit of 0.06 μM in a linear range from 0.1 to 20.0 μM. Based on this, GQDs-Ce4+ paper-based sensors were also prepared for the visual detection of PO4 3-, which showed good responses to PO4 3-. This development is expected to be a promising strategy for convenient and rapid phosphate detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), newcomers of the nanocarbon materials family, are made of single or few graphene lattices, zero-dimensional nanometer-sized graphene pieces and exhibit outstanding optical and electronic properties [1–3]. Owing to their tunable florescence (FL), excellent water solubility, low toxicity, biological compatibility and facile functionalization, GQDs have tremendous advantages in the competition with traditional quantum dots [4,5]. As a result, studying on high quality GQDs is highly necessary for many applications such as fluorescence sensors [6–9], bioimaging [10], photocatalysis [11], solar cells [12], light-emitting diodes [13] and drug delivery [14]. In this spirit, all kinds of efforts have been used to efficiently prepare GQDs with high crystallinity, mainly involving two strategies which are “top down” and “bottom up” methods. Typically, the top-down method is generally using large sp2 carbon domain such as graphite, carbon fibers [15], carbon nanotubes [16], or graphene oxide [17] to synthesize GQDs. The reaction mechanisms are mainly related to the oxidation process (etching or cutting) or mechanical shearing [18,19]. However, this method usually causes low quantum yield and poor optical properties. Meanwhile, the morphologies, sizes and defects of GQDs are difficult to control through rigid cutting, and the precursors are only limited to the bulk carbon materials.

Given this, the “bottom-up” method has brought tremendous attentions because of its easy and facile preparation process. Typically, the bottom-up method is to synthesize GQDs from small organic molecules containing phenyl compounds under hydrothermal or solvothermal treatment [20,21]. The main reaction mechanisms are involving dehydrogenation and carbonization progresses [22]. This technique usually can get high quality GQDs, however the starting materials generally are highly of toxicity, which can cause serious pollution to the environment [10]. Briefly, the bottom-up method is considered to be a good option to synthesize high quality GQDs, but those disadvantages of precursors are needed to be solved. Recently, biomass such as rice husk [19,22], plant contraction [23], lignin [10], durian [21], dead leaves [24] and molasses [25] have served as precursor to synthesize GQDs, but among them, GQDs with high quantum yield can be rarely obtained directly from biomass and researchers always improve the quantum yield of GQDs by doping heteroatoms into GQDs, which made the synthesis process of GQDs more complex. Consequently, a facile synthesis process and eco-friendly, green, non-toxic starting material is urgently desired to synthesize high quantum yield GQDs.

Due to the excellent photoluminescence properties of GQDs, it has been applied to ions detection, such as Fe3+, Pb2+, Ag+, Hg2+ [26–29]. Phosphorus, an essential element for the growth of plants and animals, is indispensable to the production of crop and animal. Phosphate (PO4 3-) is one of the main forms of phosphorus in natural water bodies. Due to soil erosion and agricultural fertilization, phosphate (PO4 3-) can cause the eutrophication of water bodies, increase toxic and harmful substances in water bodies, and reduce water quality. Therefore, its control and detection are of importance in freshwater eutrophication. Recently, various fluorescent probes have been developed for the detection of PO4 3-, such as organic dyes [30], semiconductor quantum dots (QD) [31] and carbon dots (CD) [32,33]. And during the detection process, HPO4 2- and H2PO4 - would not interfere the detection procedure of PO4 3- [34,36]. However, a few reports studied on GQDs-based probes for the detection of PO4 3-. Bai et al. [34] used GQDs and Eu3+ to construct a probe to detect PO4 3- with a detect limit 0.1 μM. Chai et al. [35] found that S-GQDs can be quenched by Dy3+, causing aggregation of GQDs, and the fluorescence of the S-GQDs gradually recovered with adding the PO4 3- to the S-GQDs-Dy3+ system. They used S-GQDs and rare earth element Dy3+ to build an “off-to-on” fluorescent probe to detect the PO4 3- in water and the lowest detection limit was 0.1 μM. Wang et al. [36] had used the system Mo7O24 6- and N-GQDs to detect PO4 3- in fluorescence “off-to-on” mode with a detect limit 0.05 μM. Chen et al. [33] reported a new type of probe (on-to-off) for detection of PO4 3-, which used Al3+ to enhance the fluorescence of S-GQDs firstly, and then used the PO4 3- to quench the fluorescence of S-GQDs-Al3+, and the detect limit was 0.1 μM. Those probes they built were well suited for PO4 3- detection, however, the GQDs they used almost were doped graphene quantum dots and the related ions they used to build GQDs fluorescent probes were too expensive or hard to obtain. Hence, a green, facile, economical, and low detection limit strategy is needed to be developed for detecting PO4 3-.

Herein biomass corn straw powders were proposed to directly synthesize GQDs through a facile, one pot hydrothermal method for the first time. Optical properties and chemical structures of the as-prepared GQDs were widely characterized by using various methods. Based on the excellent photoluminescence, a non-doped GQDs, facile and convenient fluorescent probe (GQDs-Ce4+ or GQDs-Fe3+) was proposed to detect PO4 3-.

The corn straw of this study was collected from rural areas in Shandong Province, China. KCl, CuSO4·5H2O, FeCl3, CaCl2, ZnSO4·7H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, CdCl2, Pb (NO3)2, CoCl2·6H2O were all purchased from Beijing Lanyi Chemical Products Co. Ltd (Beijing, China). Sodium phosphate were purchased from Beijing Boen Kechuang Technology Co., Ltd. Lanthanum(III) chloride, samarium(III) nitrate hexahydrate (Sm(NO3)3·6H2O), thulium nitrate hexahydrate (H12N3O15Tm), europium nitrate hexahydrate (EuH12N3O15), and gadolinium nitrate hexahydrate (Gd (NO3)3·6H2O) were all purchased from Ailin (Shanghai) Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. Cerium(Ⅲ) nitrate hexahydrate, quinine sulfate dehydrates and ammonium ceric nitrate (H8CeN8O18) were all purchased from Beijing Merida Technology Co., Ltd (Beijing, China).

The corn straw was washed, dried and grounded to powders (sieved with 100 mesh). The corn straw powders were washed by HCl (0.1 M) and thoroughly rinsed with deionized (DI) water 3 times, then dried in oven at 80 °C for 12 h. A sample of 300 mg corn straw powders together with 50 mL of DI water was placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 170 °C for 12 h ( Scheme 1a). After cooling down to room temperature, the product containing water-soluble GQDs were centrifuged at 9280g force for 30 min to obtain the supernatant, after that, the supernatants were further filtered by a 0.22 µm microporous membrane to purify GQDs. The purified GQDs were dried in vacuum freeze-drying machine (BIOCOOL, FD-1A-50+) before characterizing and measuring.

The GQDs solution with a concentration of 0.1 mg/mL at pH of 4 was prepared initially. Various of metal ion salt solutions (KCl, CuSO4·5H2O, FeCl3, CaCl2, ZnSO4·7H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, CdCl2, Pb(NO3)2, CoCl2·6H2O, H8CeN8O18, Sm(NO3)3·6H2O, H12N3O15Tm, EuH12N3O15, Gd(NO3)3·6H2O) were freshly prepared with a concentration of 1 mM. To evaluate the fluorescence quenching effect of metal ions on GQDs, 1.5 mL of metal ion salt solutions and 1.5 mL of GQDs solution were added to a 5 mL centrifuge tube. After 15 min incubation at room temperature, the fluorescent spectra were recorded on a fluorescence spectrophotometer with an optimal excitation wavelength of 370 nm.

Based on the results of 2.3.1, the as-prepared GQDs can be quenched by Ce4+ or Fe3+, and the aggregation of GQDs-Ce4+ or GQDs-Fe3+was used to build an “off-to-on” probe for detecting PO4 3- (Scheme 1b). In the typical experimental procedure, 1.5 mL 0.1 mg/mL GQDs solution was added in a few 5 mL centrifuge tubes respectively, followed by adding various concentrations of Ce4+ or Fe3+ solutions. After 15 min incubation at room temperature, the fluorescence quenching spectra were recorded on a fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength at 370 nm. Three parallel experiments were conducted for each experiment. Subsequently, under the optimal concentrations of Ce4+and Fe3+, various concentrations of PO4 3- (0–100 μM) were added into the GQDs-Ce3+ or GQDs-Fe3+solution, and the fluorescence recovery spectra of GQDs were recorded on a fluorescence spectrophotometer with an optimal excitation wavelength of 370 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of PO4 3- in the real water sample
ce:section.ce:para: The real water samples were collected from the school’s sewage treatment station (inflow and outflow of the sewage treatment station) and Xinzhuangqiao River in Beijing. All the water samples were filtered through 0.22 µm membrane filters.

Firstly, 5 mL of Ce4+ (1 mM) were added into 5 mL of GQDs (1 mg/mL) solution, and reacted at room temperature for 15 min. Subsequently, the GQDs-Ce4+ paper-based sensors were prepared by immersing the cut ordinary qualitative filter paper tapes into the GQDs-Ce4+ solution for 5 min, and then drying in an oven with 60 °C for 30 min. In a typical run, different concentrations of PO4 3- (0.1–20 μmol/L) were freshly prepared, and then 1 μL of various concentrations of PO4 3- were added into the center of GQDs-Ce4+ paper-based sensors.

The QY of GQDs was calculated based on the quinine sulfate solution (QY = 54%) in 0.1 M H2SO4 aqueous solution. The UV absorption value of all GQDs samples were adjusted to less than 0.1 to reduce the internal filtration effect. The QY of GQDs was calculated based on the following equation [37,38].
QY
2
QY
1
=
A
1
A
2
×
F
2
F
1
×
η
2
η
1
2
2
Where subscripts 1 and 2 indicate standard reference solution and sample solution respectively, QY is the quantum yield, A represents the absorbance values of samples, F is the integrated area of fluorescent intensity, and η represents the refractive index of the solvent. When the absorbance values of standard reference and sample solutions are less than 0.1, the concentrations of standard reference and sample solutions are relatively small, and the refractive indices of these two solutions have little difference, therefore, the η values of standard reference and sample solutions are all set as 1 in this study.

The ions limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by the following formula.
LOD =
3 σ
K
Where σ is the standard deviation of blank samples (measured by the blank sample n = 11). K is the slope of the linear range in the fluorescence recovering ratio graph.

The high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM-2100) and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker Multi Mode 8) were used to characterize the morphologies and thickness of the GQDs. The crystalline structure of GQDs was characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Shimadzu, XRD-7000). The functional groups of GQDs were characterized using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Bruker, Vertex-70 with an ATR accessory). The elemental and functional groups analyses were performed on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250) using Al Kalph 150 W. The ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) absorption properties of GQDs were analyzed using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV2310, TECHCOMP). The fluorescence (FL) spectra were recorded using fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, HITACHI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural properties of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM image ( Fig. 1a) obviously showed that the as-synthesized GQDs were dispersed evenly with a size distribution ranging from 1.5 to 4.0 nm. According to the histogram of size distribution, the average size of the GQDs was approximately 2.67 nm. The crystallinity of GQDs was revealed by HRTEM. The image of HRTEM (Fig. 1b) obviously showed that the GQDs had a hexagonal pattern, indicating highly resolved crystal structure. The black dotted cutting line (Fig. 1d) was further proved that it had a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, equaling to the (1100) lattice plane of graphene [10,39]. Fig. 1c displayed the pattern of fast Fourier transform (FFT) analysis, which had a significant symmetry of six edges, thus, high quality graphene single-crystals structure had been further verified. AFM images (Fig. 1e, f) characterized the thickness of GQDs, demonstrating the thicknesses of GQDs were 0.4–1.3 nm, indicative of 1–3 layers graphene [39]. The high crystallinity and the significant difference in lateral size and height further proved that the synthesis of GQDs was flat, rather than quasi-spherical.

The crystalline structure of GQDs was characterized by XRD spectrometer. As depicted in Fig. 2a, the GQDs exhibited a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 26°, which was the characteristic peak of graphite crystal plane (002) [40]. The observed wide peak also showed that the as-prepared GQDs had a relatively small particle size. Generally, the graphene oxide peak appeared at 2θ = 19°, which was not seen in Fig. 2a, so the prepared GQDs were pure GQDs [41]. In contrast, the corn straw powders had a broad peak in 2θ = 21°, the shifted peak can be ascribed to the conversion of amorphous corn powder to GQDs.

The FTIR were collected to characterize the chemical functional groups of GQDs. As shown in Fig. 2b, the peak in 3348 and 2912 cm−1 was attributed to the -OH and C-H stretching vibrations. The peaks in 1733, 1652 and 1018 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of CO, CC, C-O functional groups. And interestingly, the FTIR spectrum of GQDs showed the in-plane and out-plane deformations stretching vibrations of aromatic C–H at 883 and 762 cm−1 [10,35,42,43]. Those results demonstrated that the synthesized GQDs were composed of aromatic skeletons and richened in oxygen-containing functional groups. As compared with the FTIR spectrum of corn straw powders, it can be clearly seen that the stretching vibrations of oxygen-containing functional groups (-OH, CO, C-O, etc.) in FTIR spectrum of corn straw powders were weak, which further proved that the prepared GQDs contained more aromatic and oxygen-containing functional groups after hydrothermal treatment.

The element types and chemical states of the surface were measured by XPS. As depicted in Fig. 2c, the XPS survey of GQDs showed the peaks of C1s and O1s were at 284 eV and 532 eV [21,44]. As shown in the high resolution spectra of C1s (Fig. 2d), the C1s spectra can be fitted by three types of carbon, the signal peaks of ~284.6 eV, ~286.1 eV and ~288.5 eV were attributed to the sp2 hybrid carbon(C-C/CC) in graphene, sp3 (C-OH/C-O-C) hybrid carbon and carboxyl carbon (COOH/CO) [21,25,42,44]. In addition, the strong signal peak of O in the XPS survey of GQDs indicated that GQDs had plenty of oxygen-containing functional groups. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2e) was deconvoluted into three main peaks, which were centered at ~523.6 eV (CO), ~533.8 eV (C-OH/C-O-C), ~531.7 eV (COOH) respectively [21]. The similar oxygen functional groups were showed in spectrum of O 1s and C 1s. Those results showed that the corn straw powders had transformed to GQDs with abundant oxygen-containing groups during the hydrothermal synthesis of GQDs.

The UV–vis, florescence spectra were recorded to analyze the optical properties of GQDs. As clearly shown in Fig. 3a, the GQDs aqueous solution were bright, transparent and light yellow under daylight. And its aqueous solution emitted bright blue fluorescence under the 365 nm UV light (Fig. 3b). After freeze-drying the GQDs aqueous solution, as shown in Fig. 3c, brown powders of GQDs were obtained. Fig. 3d (red curve) showed the UV–vis spectrum of GQDs. The GQDs aqueous solution had an absorption peak between 250 and 300 nm, which was assigned to the π-π * electronic transition in the typical conjugated CC double bond. In addition, a broad weak absorption shoulder peak around 320 nm was observed, which can be assigned to the n-π * transition of the CO bond in GQDs. Those results were consistent with the UV–vis spectra reported in some literatures [18,34,45]. The optimal fluorescence excitation wavelength and emission wavelength were 370 nm and 450 nm, as proven by the Fig. 3d (green and blue curve), indicating that the GQDs had the strongest fluorescence and emitted bright blue light. Meanwhile, the corresponding fluorescence quantum yield (QY) can reach 15.65%, which was higher than most previously reported biomass precursors derived GQDs ( Table 1). As depicted in Fig. 3e, the emission peaks of GQDs were gradually redshifted from 437 nm to 500 nm when the excitation wavelength was altered from 330 nm to 440 nm with an increment of 10 nm. The excitation-dependent behavior can be assigned to the quantum confinement effect of GQDs, edge effects, surface defects of the carbon skeleton and the introduction of oxygen-containing functional groups [10,22,46,47]. Based on the analysis of the research results, the excitation dependent behavior GQDs in this research may be attributed to the non-uniformity of the emission sites on the emission surface caused by the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the GQDs.

In order to apply the GQDs to metal ions and PO4 3- detection, the effect of pH on fluorescence intensity of GQDS was analyzed. As depicted in Fig. 3f, the normalized fluorescence intensity reached the highest at pH = 4, which was the initial prepared GQDs aqueous solution. Notably, as the pH increased from 4 to 9, the normalized fluorescence intensity of GQDs decreased. This can be attributed to the existence of abundant oxygen-containing groups on the surface of GQDs, and the pH of the solvent can affect the fluorescence properties of GQDs [25,37]. Consequently, the decrease in the fluorescence intensity of GQDs may be due to the decrease in the number of carboxyl-rich GQDs and its conversion to hydroxyl-rich GQDs [54]. And in the following experiments, the initial pH = 4 was chosen to detect the metal ions and PO4 3-.

In order to build an “off-to-on” fluorescent probe system for detecting phosphate, firstly, metal ions that can quench the fluorescence of GQDs are needed to be selected. Fig. 4a showed the fluorescence intensities of GQDs in the presence of different metal ions at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. It can be seen that with the addition of various of metal ion salts solutions, the florescence of GQDs quenched to different degrees. The lower F/F0 ratio in the presence and absence of metal ions showed the high specificity of GQDs to metal ions. Therefore, the selectivity of GQDs to Fe3+ and Ce4+ were remarkably greater than other metal ions, and the fluorescence displayed severe quenching, and Fe3+ and Ce4+ were chosen to study further. Fig. 4b was the normalized fluorescence spectra of GQDs added with various concentrations of Ce4+ (0–2 mM). With the progressive addition of Ce4+, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs gradually quenched, which can be ascribed to the coordination between Ce4+and the carboxylate groups on the GQDs, resulting in the aggregation of GQDs [37,51]. AFM was used to further measure the aggregation of GQDs-Ce4+, the results showed that the thickness of GQDs-Ce4+ was 5–37 nm. As compared to GQDs, the thickness was increased from approximately 0.4 nm to about 37 nm with the addition of Ce4+ (Fig. 1f, h), which further proved Ce4+ can induce the aggregation of GQDs. As shown in Fig. 4c, the normalized fluorescence spectra of GQDs quenched sharply with the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ (0–2 mM), which can be assigned to the specific affinity between Fe3+ and oxygen functional groups (hydroxyl groups). Due to the different bond lengths of the metal ions, the order of the stability constants of the metal ion complexes is as follows: Fe3+ > Ce4+ >Cu2+ > Zn2+ > Ni2+ > Cd2+ > Co2+ >Fe2+>Ce3+> Ca2+> K+ [22,37,52,53]. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) is one of the most accurate methods for determining the LUMO/HOMO and band gap energy values of various materials. Fig. 5 showed the current-voltage curves of GQDs, GQDs-Ce4+, GQDs-Fe3+, Ce4+-PO4 3- and Fe3+-PO4 3-. The potentials were referenced versus ferrocene/ferrocenium pair as standard to determine the Eox or Ered. The ELUMO and EHOMO were calculated based on the following empirical equations and the value of E(ferrocene/ferrocenium) was 0.31 in the equation [57].
EHOMO = -[(Eox – E(ferrocene/ferrocenium)) + 4.8] eV
ELUMO = -[(Ered – E(ferrocene/ferrocenium)) + 4.8] eV

Based on the CV results, the Eox of GQDs, GQDs-Ce4+, GQDs-Fe3+, Ce4+-PO4 3- and Fe3+-PO4 3- were 1.02, 1.01, 1.00, 0.89, 0.72 and the corresponding EHOMO were -5.51, -5.50, -5.49, -5.38, -5.21 eV, respectively. The Ered of GQDs, GQDs-Ce4+, GQDs-Fe3+, Ce4+-PO4 3- and Fe3+-PO4 3- were 0.45, 0.46, 0.47, 0.54, 0.34, and thus, the ELUMO were -4.94, -4.95, -4.96, -5.03, -4.83 eV. According to the obtained information, LUMO/HOMO gap (Eg) were calculated, which were directly correlated with the charge of metal ions on GQDs surface. The lower Eg is caused by the effect of perfect hybridization between metal ion and GQDs [56]. The lower Eg favor for the charge transfer between the complexes and can form perfect hybridization structure, quenching the fluorescence of GQDs. Table 2 showed that the Eg of GQDs-Fe3+ and GQDs-Ce4+ was lower than that of GQDs, which meant Fe3+ and Ce4+ can better hybridize with GQDs and quench the fluorescence of GQDs. Table 2 also showed that the Eg of GQDs-Fe3+ was slightly lower than that of GQDs-Ce4+, indicating that the hybridization between Fe3+ and GQDs was better than that of Ce4+, resulting in a better fluorescence quenching effect on GQDs. These results were in good agreement with our metal ions sensing experiment. When the concentration of Fe3+ reached 1 mM, the fluorescence almost quenched to 80%, therefore, the optimal concentration of the “off-to-on” fluorescent probe system constructed with these two metal ions (GQDs-Ce4+/GQDs-Fe3+) was 1 mM.

Iron is one of the common metal elements in the environment and has a potential to react with phosphate to form vivianite [37]. Ce4+, a rare-earth metal ion, has a good affinity with the oxygen-donor atoms of carboxylate groups and can be used as a choice of phosphorus affinity materials [51]. In our work, we found that in the presence of PO4 3-, the aggregation and quenching effect of GQDs caused by Ce4+ can be inhibited since the affinity between Ce4+ and the oxygen donor atom of PO4 3- was higher than the carboxylate group of Ce4+ and GQDs [34,35]. As depicted in Fig. 6a, with adding the PO4 3- into the GQDs-Ce4+ aggregates, the fluorescence of the solution recovered progressively. This was due to the competition for Ce4+ ions between the oxygen-donating atoms from PO4 3- and the carboxylate groups of GQDs, leading to the desorption of Ce4+ from the surface of GQDs, which was similar with the result of some literratures [34,35]. Fig. 6b displayed the linear relationship between F/F0 values and PO4 3- concentrations (0–100 μM) of GQDs-Ce4+ probes. When the PO4 3- concentration reached to 20 μM, the fluorescence recovery of the GQDs solution equilibrated gradually and the fluorescnce intensity of GQDs was almost completely recovered. The inset of Fig. 6b was the linear relationship at lower concentrations of PO4 3- (0–20 μM). In the PO4 3- concentration of 0.1–2 μM and 2–20 μM, it fitted well with two linear equations with correlation coefficient R 2 = 0.993 and R 2 = 0.984 respectively, demonstrating a good linearity between F/F0 value and the concentration of PO4 3-. The LOD of PO4 3-was caculated to be 0.06 μM in a range of 0.1–20 μM under 3 times of the standard deviation rule, which was lower than most literatures ( Table 3) [34,35]. Similarly, the phosphate was also detected by the fluorescent probe system GQDs-Fe3+. As Fig. 6c and Fig. 6d depicted, with adding the PO4 3- into the GQDs-Fe3+, the fluorescence of solution was recovered gradually, and the LOD of PO4 3- was caculated to be 0.09 μM in a range of 0.1–1.4 μM under 3 times of the standard deviation rule. However the fluorescence was not compeleted recovered as compared with the GQDs-Ce4+ (Fig. 6a and c). This may be caused by the stability constants of the metal ion complexes, the stability of metal ion complex in Fe3+ was gerater than Ce4+ [52]. Therefore, the ability of the oxygen-donor atoms of PO4 3- combining with the Ce4+ of GQDs-Ce4+ was greater than the Fe3+ of GQDs-Fe3+, resulting in the desorption of Ce4+ of the surface of GQDs-Ce4+ was more than the desorption of Fe3+ of GQDs-Fe3+, so the fluorescence recovery of the former was stronger. As shown in Table 2, it clearly showed that the Eg of Ce4+-PO4 3-/Fe3+-PO4 3- was lower than that of GQDs-Ce4+/GQDs-Fe3+, which meant when PO4 3- existed, Ce4+/Fe3+can be desorbed from GQDs to form more stable hybridization structure with PO4 3-. However, the Eg of Fe3+-PO4 3- was higher than that of Ce4+-PO4 3-, which meant Fe3+-PO4 3- was less stable than Ce4+-PO4 3-, resulting poor fluorescence recovery of GQDs-Fe3+ when PO4 3- existed. In addition, the Eg of GQDs-Fe3+ was lower than GQDs-Ce4+, which also meant GQDs-Fe3+ was more stable than GQDs-Ce4+, consequently, the desorption of Fe3+ from GQDs-Fe3+ was harder than the desorption of Ce4+ from GQDs-Ce4+, which was consistent with the experiment results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of PO4 3- in the real water sample
ce:section.ce:para: Table 4 showed the comparison of three methods (UV spectrophotometer measurement, GQDs-Fe3+ fluorescent probe, GQDs-Ce4+ fluorescent probe) for detecting of PO4 3- in real water samples [55]. It can be seen from Table 4 that the results of the three methods for PO4 3- detection were similar and the calculated relative errors (RE) were all under 25%. This result further proved that the two fluorescent probe systems can be applied as a rapid detection method for the detection of PO4 3- in water bodies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PO4 3- by GQDs-Ce4+ paper-based sensors
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 showed the fluorescence recovery of the GQDs-Ce4+ paper-based sensors by adding 1 μL of various concentrations of PO4 3- into the center of GQDs-Ce4+ paper-based sensors under 365 nm ultraviolet lamp irradiating. It can be seen from the Fig. 6 that as the concentration of PO4 3- increased, the fluorescence color in the middle of the GQDs-Ce4+ paper-based sensors gradually altered from dark to blue. When the concentration of PO4 3- reached to 1.4 μM, the fluorescence color in the middle of the GQDs-Ce4+ paper-based sensor had an obvious recovery and can be clearly observed by naked eyes. When the PO4 3- concentration reached to 20 μM, the fluorescence color in the middle of GQDs-Ce4+ paper-based sensor had basically returned to its original fluorescence, exhibiting bright blue. This was consistent with the result of detecting the PO4 3- by using GQDs-Ce4+ on in the previous section. The GQDs-Ce4+ paper-based sensors had advantages of portability and easy operation and can broaden the detection of PO4 3- in the future and make the detection of PO4 3- simple and convenient.

In summary, high quantum yield GQDs was directly prepared using corn straw powders by one-pot hydrothermal treatment for the first time. The as-prepared GQDs showed an average particle diameter about 2.67 nm with good water dispersibility, and abundant carboxyl groups on the surface than the original precursor. The florescence of GQDs can be quenched by Ce4+ and Fe3+, based on this, a non-doped GQDs probe system (GQDs-Ce4+ or GQDs-Fe3+) was built to detect PO4 3- under an “off-to-on” mode. It was showed that the “off-to-on” probe system (GQDs-Ce4+ or GQDs-Fe3+) was an efficient fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of PO4 3- and had a low LOD of 0.06 μM or 0.09 μM respectively. GQDs-Ce4+ paper-based sensors were also prepared for the visual detection of PO4 3-, and which had a good response to PO4 3- in a PO4 3- concentration of 0.1–20 μM. This research offered a method for synthesizing high quantum yield GQDs from cheap, renewable, abundant, and environmental-friendly lignocellulosic biomass. At the same time, it also proposed a cost-effective, facile fluorescent probe approach to detect the PO4 3- and broaden the application prospect of PO4 3- detection.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52070016) and National Key Research and Development Program of China (2016YFC0207907).

Chem. Eng. J.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simple and cost-effective synthesis of fluorescent graphene quantum dots from honey: application as stable security ink and white-light emission
Part. Part. Syst. Charact.

Particle & Particle Systems Characterization 2015, 33 (2), 70–74.

Rsc Adv.
